title: Controlled Vocabulary: Story Element Relationship Types
description: Canonical set of inter-character relationship types used across element templates and instances. Every relationship between characters must be assigned exactly one type. Relationships are directional — 'mentor_student' from A to B means A mentors B. These types describe the narrative function of the bond, not its emotional quality.

relationship_types.definition: Characters who actively support each other's goals through cooperation, loyalty, or shared purpose.
relationship_types.structural_function: Provides mutual aid during Trial and Crisis phases. Ally loss raises stakes; ally gain provides resources.
relationship_types.typical_context: Companions on a quest, fellow rebels, friends working toward a common goal
relationship_types.directionality: Typically symmetric — if A is ally to B, B is ally to A

relationship_types.definition: Characters competing for the same goal, status, or resource — opposition driven by ambition rather than malice.
relationship_types.structural_function: Drives conflict through competition rather than existential threat. May evolve into ally or escalate to nemesis.
relationship_types.typical_context: Two suitors, competing heirs, professional rivals, opposing champions

relationship_types.definition: A relationship where one character guides, teaches, or trains the other — transferring knowledge, skill, or wisdom.
relationship_types.structural_function: Accelerates protagonist development. Mentor often appears at Catalyst phase and may exit before Crisis, forcing independence.
relationship_types.typical_context: Master and apprentice, teacher and pupil, experienced guide and newcomer
relationship_types.directionality: Asymmetric — the mentor teaches, the student learns

relationship_types.definition: A familial bond (biological or adopted) carrying inherent obligation, expectation, and emotional weight.
relationship_types.structural_function: Creates deep stakes through primal bonds. Parental loss or failure is among the most potent story catalysts.
relationship_types.typical_context: Mother/father and son/daughter, adoptive parent, surrogate family figure

relationship_types.definition: Characters bound by romantic love, desire, or intimate partnership — creating vulnerability through emotional exposure.
relationship_types.structural_function: Raises personal stakes beyond plot-level conflict. Romantic bonds can motivate sacrifice, create liability, or provide resolution.
relationship_types.typical_context: Lovers, partners, unrequited love, forbidden romance, star-crossed pairs

relationship_types.definition: Characters locked in fundamental opposition — the relationship defines both characters and cannot be resolved without one prevailing.
relationship_types.structural_function: Drives the central conflict to Crisis. The nemesis relationship is the story's engine. Resolution requires confrontation.
relationship_types.typical_context: Hero and villain, detective and criminal mastermind, protagonist and their dark mirror
relationship_types.directionality: Typically symmetric — each defines the other

relationship_types.definition: A relationship of authority and obedience — one character commands, the other serves (willingly or under coercion).
relationship_types.structural_function: Creates power dynamics that can invert at reversal points. Servant rebellion or master vulnerability are potent story moments.
relationship_types.typical_context: Lord and vassal, employer and employee, enslaver and enslaved, commander and soldier

relationship_types.definition: Characters who share a familial bond as equals — brothers, sisters, or equivalent kinship carrying shared history and loyalty.
relationship_types.structural_function: Creates bonds with inherent equality and rivalry. Sibling conflict carries unique emotional weight due to shared origin.
relationship_types.typical_context: Brothers, sisters, twins, sworn siblings, characters raised together

relationship_types.definition: A character who violates an existing bond of trust — breaking an alliance, revealing a secret, or switching sides.
relationship_types.structural_function: Drives Reversal and Revelation nodes. Betrayal recontextualizes prior events and destroys established trust.
relationship_types.typical_context: The trusted ally who was a spy, the friend who sells out, the partner who defects
relationship_types.directionality: Asymmetric — one betrays, one is betrayed

relationship_types.definition: A character charged with protecting another — through duty, love, or oath — often at personal cost.
relationship_types.structural_function: Creates stakes through protective bonds. Guardian failure or sacrifice is a powerful catalyst for protagonist action.
relationship_types.typical_context: Bodyguard, protector, watcher, keeper, parent in protective role
relationship_types.directionality: Asymmetric — one protects, one is protected
